text: Based primarily on the works of John W. Dankanich, O’Reilly et al., and Miller et al.

title: Different Definitions of the Sphere of Influence (SOI)
text:
2.1 Formula
\[
R_{SOI}
=
a\left(\frac{m_p}{m_s}\right)^{2/5}
\]
where

\(a\) = orbital radius of the planet about the Sun
\(m_p\) = mass of the planet
\(m_s\) = mass of the Sun

title: Different Definitions of the Sphere of Influence (SOI)
text:
2.2 Physical Meaning
The Laplace SOI is not the distance where the Sun’s gravity equals the planet’s gravity.
Instead, it is the distance where

the planet-centered two-body approximation becomes more accurate than the heliocentric two-body approximation.

This definition is obtained by comparing
\[
\frac{p_s}{a_p}
\]
and
\[
\frac{P_p}{A_s}
\]
and setting them equal.

title: Different Definitions of the Sphere of Influence (SOI)
text:
2.3 Applications

Patched-conic approximation
Interplanetary trajectory design
Mission analysis
Curtis
Bate, Mueller & White







Important



The Laplace SOI is a model-selection boundary, not a physical boundary.

title: Different Definitions of the Sphere of Influence (SOI)
text:
3.1 Formula
\[
R_H
=
a
\left(
\frac{m_p}{3m_s}
\right)^{1/3}
\]
Notice

exponent = \(\frac13\)
factor = 3

title: Different Definitions of the Sphere of Influence (SOI)
text:
3.2 Physical Meaning
The Hill Sphere represents the region where

a satellite can remain gravitationally bound to the planet despite the gravitational influence of the Sun.

Unlike Laplace’s SOI, this has a genuine dynamical interpretation.

title: Different Definitions of the Sphere of Influence (SOI)
text:
3.3 Example
For Earth,

Laplace SOI

\[
\approx 925,000\text{ km}
\]

Hill Sphere

\[
\approx 1.5\times10^6\text{ km}
\]
The Hill Sphere is considerably larger.

title: Different Definitions of the Sphere of Influence (SOI)
text:
3.4 Applications

Satellite stability
Natural satellites
Lagrange points
CR3BP
Long-term orbital dynamics

title: Different Definitions of the Sphere of Influence (SOI)
text:
4.1 Formula
Starting with
\[
\frac{GM_p}{r^2}
=
\frac{GM_s}{R^2}
\]
gives
\[
r
=
R
\sqrt{\frac{m_p}{m_s}}
\]

title: Different Definitions of the Sphere of Influence (SOI)
text:
4.2 Physical Meaning
At this distance,
\[
F_{\text{planet}}
=
F_{\text{Sun}}
\]

title: Different Definitions of the Sphere of Influence (SOI)
text:
4.3 Example
For Earth,
\[
r
\approx
259,000\text{ km}
\]
which is much smaller than the Laplace SOI.

title: Different Definitions of the Sphere of Influence (SOI)
text:
4.4 Why isn’t it used?
Although the forces become equal,
this does not imply equal orbital influence.
The planet and spacecraft are both accelerating toward the Sun, so most of the Sun’s gravity cancels when studying motion relative to the planet.
Only the differential (tidal) acceleration affects the relative motion.
Therefore, the equal-force radius has limited usefulness in orbital mechanics.






Warning



Equal gravitational force does not imply equal orbital influence.

title: Different Definitions of the Sphere of Influence (SOI)
text:
5.1 Formula
\[
R_R
\approx
2.44R_p
\left(
\frac{\rho_p}{\rho_s}
\right)^{1/3}
\]
where

\(R_p\) = radius of the primary body
\(\rho_p\) = density of the primary
\(\rho_s\) = density of the satellite

title: Different Definitions of the Sphere of Influence (SOI)
text:
5.2 Physical Meaning
Inside the Roche Limit,
tidal forces exceed the self-gravity of the satellite, causing it to break apart.

title: Different Definitions of the Sphere of Influence (SOI)
text:
5.3 Applications

Planetary rings
Tidal disruption
Comets
Planetary science

title: Different Definitions of the Sphere of Influence (SOI)
text:
6.1 Applications

Asteroid capture
Planet formation
Temporary gravitational capture

title: Different Definitions of the Sphere of Influence (SOI)
text:
8.1 Applications

High-fidelity trajectory propagation
NASA
ESA
JPL mission design

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text: Based primarily on the works of John W. Dankanich, O’Reilly et al., and Miller et al.

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
2.1 Definition
Low-thrust propulsion refers to propulsion systems producing accelerations much smaller than gravitational acceleration.
Typically:
\[
a_T \ll g_0
\]
where
\[
g_0 = 9.81 \, m/s^2
\]
Typical thrust levels:



System
Thrust




Chemical Rocket
\(10^3 - 10^7\) N


Hall Thruster
0.01–5 N


Ion Thruster
0.001–1 N


Electrospray
μN–mN


FEEP
μN

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
2.2 Rocket Thrust Equation
The general thrust equation is
\[
T=\dot m v_e +(P_e-P_a)A_e
\]
where

\(T\) = thrust
\(\dot m\) = mass flow rate
\(v_e\) = exhaust velocity
\(P_e\) = exit pressure
\(P_a\) = ambient pressure
\(A_e\) = nozzle exit area

For electric propulsion operating in vacuum,
\[
T \approx \dot m v_e
\]

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
2.3 Specific Impulse
Specific impulse is a measure of propellant efficiency.
\[
I_{sp}=\frac{v_e}{g_0}
\]
Higher specific impulse means:

Less propellant consumption
Greater mission capability
Increased payload fraction

Typical values:



Propulsion Type
Specific Impulse (\(I_{sp}\))




Chemical
250–450 s


Resistojet
300–500 s


Arcjet
500–1000 s


Hall Thruster
1000–2500 s


Ion Thruster
2000–10000 s


FEEP
>10000 s

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
A. Electrothermal Propulsion
Electrical energy heats the propellant before expansion through a nozzle.

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
B. Electrostatic Propulsion
Electric fields accelerate ions directly.

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
C. Electromagnetic Propulsion
Electric and magnetic fields accelerate plasma.

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
D. Advanced Concepts

Solar sails
Nuclear electric propulsion
Electrospray propulsion
Hybrid propulsion systems

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
Principle
Electrical energy is used to increase the thermal energy of the propellant.
The heated gas expands through a conventional nozzle to produce thrust.
\[
T \propto \dot m v_e
\]
Electrothermal systems generally provide moderate specific impulse with relatively simple designs.

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
5.1 Resistojets

Working Principle
Electrical resistance heaters raise the propellant temperature.
The heated gas expands through a converging-diverging nozzle.


Common Propellants

Nitrogen
Ammonia
Water vapor
Xenon



Advantages

Simple design
High reliability
Low cost
Flight heritage



Disadvantages

Limited performance
Lower specific impulse than other electric propulsion systems



Typical Performance



Parameter
Value




Thrust
mN–N


Specific Impulse
300–500 s

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
5.2 Arcjets

Working Principle
An electrical arc heats the propellant to extremely high temperatures.
The superheated gas then expands through a nozzle.


Advantages

Higher performance than resistojets
Mature technology



Disadvantages

Electrode erosion
Increased power requirements



Typical Performance



Parameter
Value




Specific Impulse
500–1000 s


Thrust
0.01–1 N





Applications

Geostationary satellite station keeping
Orbit transfer

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
6.1 Gridded Ion Thrusters

Working Principle

Neutral xenon gas is ionized.
Positive ions are extracted.
Electrostatic grids accelerate the ions.

The exhaust velocity is
\[
v_e = \sqrt{\frac{2qV}{m}}
\]
where:

\(q\) = ion charge
\(V\) = accelerating voltage
\(m\) = ion mass



Advantages

Extremely high efficiency
High specific impulse
Excellent for deep-space missions



Disadvantages

Low thrust levels
Grid erosion
Complex power processing requirements



Typical Performance



Parameter
Value




Specific Impulse
2000–10000 s


Thrust
mN–N





Applications

Deep Space 1
Dawn
BepiColombo

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
6.2 Hall Effect Thrusters

Working Principle
A radial magnetic field traps electrons inside the discharge chamber.
The Lorentz force acting on charged particles is
\[
\mathbf{F}=q(\mathbf{E}+\mathbf{v}\times\mathbf{B})
\]
The trapped electrons ionize the propellant, while ions are accelerated axially to produce thrust.


Advantages

Higher thrust density than ion thrusters
Simpler architecture
Mature technology



Disadvantages

Lower specific impulse than gridded ion thrusters
Channel erosion



Typical Performance



Parameter
Value




Specific Impulse
1000–2500 s


Thrust
10 mN–5 N





Applications

GEO satellites
Orbit raising
Constellation maintenance

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
6.3 Electrospray Thrusters

Working Principle
Electrostatic forces extract charged droplets or ions from a conductive liquid.
A Taylor cone forms at the emitter tip.
The electric field is approximately
\[
E = \frac{V}{d}
\]
where:

\(V\) = applied voltage
\(d\) = emitter spacing



Advantages

Extremely compact
Suitable for CubeSats
Scalable architecture



Disadvantages

Limited thrust
Manufacturing complexity



Typical Performance



Parameter
Value




Thrust
μN–mN


Specific Impulse
500–3000 s

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
6.4 FEEP Thrusters

Field Emission Electric Propulsion
FEEP thrusters accelerate ions extracted directly from liquid metals.


Typical Propellants

Indium
Cesium



Advantages

Extremely high precision
Very high specific impulse



Applications

Precision formation flying
Drag-free spacecraft
Scientific missions



Typical Performance



Parameter
Value




Specific Impulse
4000–12000 s


Thrust
μN

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
7.1 Pulsed Plasma Thrusters (PPT)

Working Principle
A capacitor discharges through a solid Teflon propellant.
The resulting plasma is accelerated electromagnetically.


Advantages

Very simple
Compact
Proven technology



Disadvantages

Low efficiency
Limited thrust



Typical Performance



Parameter
Value




Specific Impulse
500–2000 s


Thrust
μN–mN

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
7.2 Magnetoplasmadynamic Thrusters (MPD)

Working Principle
Plasma carrying electrical current interacts with magnetic fields.
The resulting Lorentz force accelerates the plasma.
\[
\mathbf{F}=\mathbf{J}\times\mathbf{B}
\]
where:

\(\mathbf{J}\) = current density
\(\mathbf{B}\) = magnetic field



Advantages

Very high thrust
Very high specific impulse



Disadvantages

Extremely high power requirements

Typical power levels:
\[
100\,kW - 1\,MW
\]


Potential Applications

Human Mars missions
Cargo transport
Deep-space exploration

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
8.1 Solar Sails

Principle
Solar sails utilize momentum transfer from sunlight.
The solar radiation pressure is
\[
P=\frac{2S}{c}
\]
where:

\(S\) = solar flux
\(c\) = speed of light



Advantages

No propellant required
Unlimited mission duration



Disadvantages

Extremely low acceleration
Large sail structures required

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
8.2 Nuclear Electric Propulsion (NEP)

Principle
A nuclear reactor generates electrical power.
The electricity drives electric thrusters.


Advantages

High available power
Deep-space capability
Independent of solar distance



Applications

Mars missions
Outer planet exploration
Cargo transportation

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
10.1 Direct Methods
The trajectory is divided into multiple segments.
Optimization variables include:

Thrust magnitude
Thrust direction
Flight time


Advantages

Numerically robust
Suitable for complex missions
Widely used

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
10.2 Indirect Methods
Based on optimal control theory.

Pontryagin’s Maximum Principle
Provides necessary conditions for optimality.


Advantages

Highly accurate
Produces optimal solutions



Disadvantages

Sensitive to initial guesses
Difficult implementation

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
Spiral Orbit Raising
Instead of a Hohmann transfer, the spacecraft gradually spirals outward.
Applications:

LEO to GEO transfer
Orbit raising

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
Interplanetary Transfers
Continuous thrust changes:

Orbital energy
Inclination
Eccentricity

simultaneously.

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
Asteroid Rendezvous
Low-thrust propulsion allows gradual matching of an asteroid’s velocity and orbit.

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
Station Keeping
Compensates for:

Solar radiation pressure
Atmospheric drag
Gravitational perturbations

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
Orbit Raising
Used extensively by commercial communications satellites.

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
Formation Flying
Maintains precise relative positioning between spacecraft.

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
Collision Avoidance
Provides efficient orbit adjustments in congested orbital environments.

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
Deep-Space Exploration
Enabled missions such as:

SMART-1
Deep Space 1
Dawn
BepiColombo

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
14.1 Power Limitations
Electric propulsion performance depends strongly on available power.
The thrust-to-power relationship can be approximated as
\[
T=\frac{2\eta P}{v_e}
\]
where:

\(\eta\) = efficiency
\(P\) = electrical power
\(v_e\) = exhaust velocity

Higher exhaust velocity generally requires greater electrical power.

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
14.2 Thermal Management
Power electronics and plasma generation create significant thermal loads.
Challenges include:

Radiator sizing
Heat rejection
Thermal control integration

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
14.3 Lifetime
Common degradation mechanisms include:

Grid erosion
Cathode wear
Plasma-induced material degradation

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
14.4 Miniaturization
Small satellites require:

Compact power processing units
Lightweight tanks
Integrated propulsion modules

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
12.1 Why Are Gravity Assists Used?
For deep-space missions, reaching distant planets or small bodies often requires a very large heliocentric velocity change.
Examples include:

Missions to Mercury
Outer planet exploration
Asteroid rendezvous missions
Comet missions

Using chemical propulsion alone would require extremely large launch vehicles or impractically high propellant masses.
A gravity assist allows a spacecraft to exchange energy and angular momentum with a planet without expending significant onboard propellant.
In the heliocentric frame:
\[
\mathbf{V}_{\infty,in} \neq \mathbf{V}_{\infty,out}
\]
and the spacecraft can gain or lose heliocentric energy through the flyby.
The planet provides the energy change through its orbital motion around the Sun.

Benefits of Gravity Assists

Large effective ΔV without propellant expenditure
Reduced launch mass
Increased payload mass
Access to otherwise unreachable destinations
Lower mission cost

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
section: 12.2 Why Not Use Pure Chemical Propulsion?
text:
12.2 Why Not Use Pure Chemical Propulsion?
A purely chemical mission would require:

Large impulsive burns
High propellant fractions
Larger launch vehicles

From the rocket equation:
\[
\Delta V = I_{sp} g_0 \ln\left(\frac{m_0}{m_f}\right)
\]
A large ΔV requirement causes the mass ratio
\[
\frac{m_0}{m_f}
\]
to increase exponentially.
Consequently:

Payload mass decreases
Launch mass increases
Mission cost rises dramatically

For missions to Mercury, Jupiter, Saturn, or beyond, a purely chemical approach may become impractical.

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
section: 12.3 Why Not Use Gravity Assists Alone?
text:
12.3 Why Not Use Gravity Assists Alone?
Gravity assists are powerful but have limitations.
A flyby can only occur if:

The spacecraft reaches the planet.
The planetary alignment is favorable.
The encounter geometry produces the desired energy change.

Gravity assists alone cannot provide complete mission flexibility.
Challenges include:

Long waiting periods for launch windows
Restricted trajectory options
Limited control over encounter conditions

Therefore, propulsion is still required to:

Reach the flyby planet
Correct trajectory errors
Adjust flyby geometry
Perform final rendezvous operations

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
section: 12.4 Why Combine Low-Thrust Propulsion with Gravity Assists?
text:
12.4 Why Combine Low-Thrust Propulsion with Gravity Assists?
Low-thrust propulsion and gravity assists complement each other extremely well.

Gravity Assist Provides

Large energy changes
Major orbit shaping
Free heliocentric ΔV



Low-Thrust Provides

Continuous trajectory control
Precise targeting
Orbit adjustment between flybys
Flexibility in launch opportunities

The combination allows missions to achieve much larger overall velocity changes than either method alone.

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
12.5 Multiple Gravity Assist (MGA) Missions
A Multiple Gravity Assist (MGA) trajectory uses several planetary flybys.
Typical sequence:
\[
Earth \rightarrow Venus \rightarrow Venus \rightarrow Mercury
\]
or
\[
Earth \rightarrow Earth \rightarrow Jupiter \rightarrow Saturn
\]
Each flyby modifies:

Orbital energy
Orbital inclination
Orbital eccentricity
Flight direction

The cumulative effect can produce enormous heliocentric velocity changes.

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
section: 12.6 Why Low-Thrust is Especially Useful in MGA Missions
text:
12.6 Why Low-Thrust is Especially Useful in MGA Missions
Between planetary encounters, a spacecraft may spend months or years in interplanetary space.
During these phases, low-thrust propulsion can:

Modify Arrival Conditions
The arrival velocity at a planet can be adjusted gradually.
This allows:

Better flyby geometry
Improved energy gain
Greater mission flexibility




Expand Launch Windows
Pure gravity-assist missions often depend on highly specific planetary alignments.
Low-thrust propulsion can compensate for small trajectory deviations and reduce dependence on exact launch opportunities.



Reduce Required Flybys
Continuous propulsion can provide part of the required ΔV.
As a result:

Fewer planetary encounters may be needed
Mission complexity decreases




Enable Final Rendezvous
Gravity assists primarily change heliocentric velocity.
However, rendezvous requires matching the target body’s velocity.
Low-thrust propulsion is highly efficient for gradually reducing relative velocity and achieving orbit insertion.

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
12.7 Comparison with Other Mission Architectures








Method
Advantages
Limitations




Chemical Only
Fast transfers, simple operations
High propellant mass


Gravity Assist Only
Very low propellant use
Limited flexibility


Low-Thrust Only
High efficiency, flexible
Long transfer times


MGA + Low-Thrust
Extremely efficient, highly capable
Complex trajectory design



For many deep-space missions, MGA combined with low-thrust propulsion provides the best balance between:

Propellant consumption
Mission duration
Launch mass
Payload capability

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
section: 12.8 Examples of MGA and Low-Thrust Missions
text:
12.8 Examples of MGA and Low-Thrust Missions

Dawn Mission
Trajectory:
\[
Earth \rightarrow Vesta \rightarrow Ceres
\]
Used:

Ion propulsion
Continuous low-thrust spirals
Asteroid rendezvous operations

Advantages:

Multiple target visits
Large total ΔV
Minimal propellant mass




BepiColombo Mission
Trajectory:
\[
Earth \rightarrow Venus \rightarrow Venus \rightarrow Mercury \rightarrow Mercury \rightarrow Mercury
\rightarrow Mercury \rightarrow Mercury \rightarrow Mercury
\]
Uses:

Solar electric propulsion
Multiple gravity assists

Purpose:
Mercury is difficult to reach because a spacecraft must lose a large amount of heliocentric energy.
The combined MGA and electric propulsion strategy drastically reduces propellant requirements.



SMART-1 Mission
Used:

Solar electric propulsion
Lunar transfer spirals

Demonstrated the practicality of low-thrust navigation and trajectory optimization.

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
section: 12.9 Limitations of Combining Low-Thrust and MGA
text:
12.9 Limitations of Combining Low-Thrust and MGA
Despite their advantages, combined MGA-low-thrust missions face several challenges.

Long Mission Duration
Continuous low thrust results in:

Months to years of transfer time
Increased operational costs




Complex Optimization
The trajectory design problem becomes highly nonlinear.
Variables include:

Flyby altitudes
Flyby dates
Thrust direction
Thrust magnitude
Time of flight

Advanced optimal control techniques are typically required.



Navigation Accuracy Requirements
Small targeting errors can significantly affect subsequent flybys.
Therefore:

Precise navigation is required
Frequent trajectory corrections may be necessary




Power Constraints
Electric propulsion performance depends on available electrical power.
At large heliocentric distances:
\[
P \propto \frac{1}{r^2}
\]
where:

\(P\) = available solar power
\(r\) = distance from the Sun

Reduced power decreases available thrust.

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
12.10 Key Takeaway
Gravity assists provide large, essentially “free” changes in heliocentric energy, while low-thrust propulsion provides continuous and highly efficient trajectory control. Individually, each technique has significant limitations. Combined, they form one of the most powerful mission design strategies available, enabling complex interplanetary missions that would be impractical or impossible using chemical propulsion alone.
For this reason, modern deep-space missions such as Dawn, SMART-1, and BepiColombo rely heavily on the combination of multiple gravity assists and low-thrust electric propulsion.

title: Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Applications
text:
Primary Sources

Dankanich, J. W. Low-Thrust Propulsion Technologies, Mission Design, and Application.
O’Reilly, D., Herdrich, G., and Kavanagh, D. F. Electric Propulsion Methods for Small Satellites: A Review. Aerospace, 2021.
Miller, D. et al. Survey and Performance Evaluation of Small-Satellite Propulsion Technologies.
